original_abstract: This editorial critically examines the burgeoning academic discourse surrounding "decentring" and "decolonizing" in the study of European Union foreign policy. Triggered by recent controversial rhetoric from EU leadership that reinforces colonial tropes—such as the "garden versus jungle" metaphor—the authors argue that there is a fundamental and often overlooked distinction between these two intellectual projects. While the "decentring" agenda seeks to pluralize perspectives to enhance the effectiveness and legitimacy of EU external action within existing structures, it risks "recentring" Europe by treating non-European knowledge as a heuristic tool for pragmatic policy recalibration. In contrast, the "decolonizing" project is rooted in critical social theory and grassroots struggles, aiming to dismantle the material and epistemic hierarchies of "coloniality" that persist long after formal independence. Through a stylized comparison of their origins, goals, and methodologies, the authors demonstrate that decentring often prioritizes institutional efficiency and "neutral" frameworks, whereas decolonization demands radical justice, reparations, and the disruption of dominant power dynamics. Ultimately, the editorial warns against the conflation of these terms, asserting that a more inclusive EU foreign policy requires moving beyond mere provincialization toward a genuine decolonial commitment that addresses the structural continuities of colonial power.
humanized_abstract: By Jakob Surbers, Martin Dedring and Mirjam Merkel More and more often it has become common in EU studies to talk about European Union foreign policy “decentring” or “decolonising”. In a particularly thought-provoking piece the authors deal critically with the former notion by explaining the problems associated with discussing “decentring” and “decolonising” as being one and the same. What particularly triggered their reflexion was that “following Juncker’s garden is the jungle” is not an exception, but a representative of a genre of rhetorical expressions of the European union’s foreign policy, whose origins are deeply seated in colonial times and reflect even today a worldview full of prejudices. In the light of this, one has to distinguish more fundamentally than it is done actually in between the concepts of decentring and decolonising EU foreign policy. What decentring represents according to the authors is a reflection of the EU institutions’ search for options how to achieve the success and legitimacy of the EU’s common foreign and security action is supposed to enjoy among external actors, which inevitably always implies a “recentring” of Europe – by reducing foreign ideas to simple policy-making instruments serving a purpose. The concept of decolonising in contrast is understood along the lines of critical social sciences focusing on coloniality and hence rooted in the protest movements of colonised populations as well as movements supporting independence and demanding reparations, focusing on the long-lasting effects of the inequalities originating from the era of empire-building. In a highly stylised way and by way of merely sketching out the difference the three authors show by way of explanation how decentring is more concerned with finding institutionally viable and rationally grounded solutions for EU foreign policy, while attempting to “recentre” the EU, and focuses by way of instrumentalising “non-EU-knowledge” solely on procedural innovations in the form of possibly more flexible modes of thinking within the EU-institutions. Decolonising by contrast means that on top of the “coloniality” that these protest movements seek to expose EU foreign policy also aims for a radical transformation of social and economic justice along with the payment of compensation claims to victims of EU politics. The authors therefore firmly warn against the naive and superficial conflation of decentring and decolonising EU foreign policy, recommending in their place rather a more focused pursuit of decentring on a purely European level, i.e. beyond the “vernacularisation of the EU’, in the form of committing to a more profound EU foreign policy that bases itself on a Decolonial approach – and with a view to an EU which considers itself ready for the necessary transformation through providing justifiable redress of “coloniality”!
